Proxy Models for Micronutrient Deficiency

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

A Simulation-Based Tutorial Using Super Learner Ensembles

Author

Micronutrient Prediction Pipeline — Tutorial

Published

February 25, 2026

1 A. Motivation and Problem Setup

1.1 Why proxy models?

Micronutrient deficiency — insufficient serum ferritin (iron), retinol-binding protein (Vitamin A), zinc, or folate — affects billions globally, with the highest burden in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. Measuring deficiency requires a venous blood draw, cold-chain sample storage, and laboratory biochemical assay. A nationally representative biomarker survey therefore costs millions of dollars and may only be conducted once per decade.

Proxy modeling asks a different question: given cheap, widely available covariates (satellite imagery, household surveys, census data), can we train a model on the expensive biomarker data and then predict deficiency prevalence everywhere — including places and time-points never sampled?

This tutorial teaches the full pipeline: simulation, preprocessing, ensemble modeling, prevalence aggregation, uncertainty quantification, and spatial analysis.

1.2 Three prediction targets

Let \(Y_i\) be a continuous biomarker (e.g., log ferritin, µg/L) and \(D_i = \mathbf{1}[Y_i < c]\) the deficiency indicator for individual \(i\), where \(c\) is a clinical threshold. Let \(X_i\) be a vector of covariates and \(A_i \in \{1,\ldots,K\}\) the Admin1 region.

We distinguish three targets:

Target 1 — Individual continuous prediction: \[\hat{E}[Y_i \mid X_i] \quad \text{(squared-error loss)}\]

Target 2 — Individual deficiency probability: \[\hat{P}(D_i = 1 \mid X_i) \quad \text{(logistic / Brier loss)}\]

Target 3 — Admin1 area prevalence: \[p_a = E[D_i \mid A_i = a] = P(Y_i < c \mid A_i = a)\]

Warning

Ecological pitfall. A model with good individual-level AUC does not automatically yield good area-level prevalence estimates. Aggregating individual probabilities is unbiased for the plug-in estimator only if the predicted probabilities are well-calibrated. Miscalibration at the tails (where deficiency occurs) propagates directly into prevalence bias.

2 B. Simulated Data Generating Process

2.1 Design choices

We simulate a survey in a fictitious country with \(K = 12\) Admin1 regions, each containing \(C = 8\) clusters (EAs), each with \(n_c = 15\) individuals (\(N = 1{,}440\) total). This mirrors the structure of a Micronutrient Survey (MNS) or DHS-linked biomarker subsample.

2.2 The DGP for the continuous biomarker

\[ Y_i = \underbrace{\mu_{a(i)}}_{\text{Admin1 RE}} + \underbrace{S(s_{c(i)})}_{\text{spatial field}} + \underbrace{u_{c(i)}}_{\text{cluster RE}} + \underbrace{f(X_i)}_{\text{covariate effects}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_i}_{\text{noise}} \]

where:

  • \(\mu_a \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_a^2)\), \(\sigma_a = 0.4\)
  • \(S(s) \sim \mathcal{GP}(0, K_{\text{exp}})\) with \(K_{\text{exp}}(d) = \sigma_s^2 \exp(-d/\rho)\), \(\sigma_s = 0.5\), \(\rho = 0.25\)
  • \(u_c \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_c^2)\), \(\sigma_c = 0.3\)
  • \(f(X_i)\) includes malaria, wealth, WASH, age (U-shaped), supplementation
  • \(\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)\), \(\sigma_\varepsilon = 0.6\)

The deficiency indicator is \(D_i = \mathbf{1}[Y_i < -0.5]\).

Code 
df <- simulate_mn_survey(
  n_admin1            = N_ADMIN1,
  clusters_per_admin1 = CLUSTERS_PER_A1,
  n_per_cluster       = N_PER_CLUSTER,
  seed                = SEED,
  deficiency_cutoff   = DEFICIENCY_CUT
)

cat("Dimensions:", nrow(df), "×", ncol(df), "\n")
#> Dimensions: 1440 × 20
Code 
cat("National deficiency prevalence:",
    round(mean(df$D) * 100, 1), "%\n")
#> National deficiency prevalence: 35.3 %
Code 
cat("Admin1 regions:", length(unique(df$admin1_name)), "\n")
#> Admin1 regions: 12
Code 
cat("Clusters:", length(unique(df$cluster_id)), "\n")
#> Clusters: 96
Code 
# Admin1-level true prevalence
true_prev <- true_admin1_prevalence(df, "admin1_name")
cat("Admin1 prevalence range:",
    round(min(true_prev) * 100, 1), "% –",
    round(max(true_prev) * 100, 1), "%\n")
#> Admin1 prevalence range: 10 % – 71.7 %
Code 
# Biomarker distribution
p_hist <- ggplot(df, aes(x = Y)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = DEFICIENCY_CUT, colour = "firebrick",
             linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = DEFICIENCY_CUT + 0.05, y = Inf,
           label = "  Deficiency\n  threshold", vjust = 1.5,
           colour = "firebrick", size = 3.5, hjust = 0) +
  labs(x = "Y (log biomarker)", y = "Count",
       title = "Simulated biomarker distribution",
       subtitle = paste0(round(mean(df$D) * 100, 1),
                         "% below threshold (deficient)"))

p_prev <- ggplot(data.frame(admin1 = names(true_prev),
                             prev   = as.numeric(true_prev)),
                 aes(x = reorder(admin1, prev), y = prev * 100)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.8) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "True prevalence (%)",
       title = "True Admin1 deficiency prevalences")

p_hist + p_prev

2.3 Survey weights

Simulated inclusion probabilities are inversely related to WASH access (rural clusters undersampled). Weighted prevalence corrects this:

Code 
prev_unwt  <- mean(df$D)
prev_wt    <- weighted.mean(df$D, df$svy_weight)
cat(sprintf("Unweighted national prevalence: %.1f%%\n", prev_unwt * 100))
#> Unweighted national prevalence: 35.3%
Code 
cat(sprintf("Weighted   national prevalence: %.1f%%\n", prev_wt   * 100))
#> Weighted   national prevalence: 35.3%

3 C. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

3.1 Missingness

We introduce MCAR missingness in ses_educ and clim_temp (5%), and MAR missingness in dis_malaria and wash_sanitation (up to 12%, higher for lower-SES individuals).

Code 
df_miss <- introduce_missingness(
  df,
  mcar_vars = c("ses_educ", "clim_temp"),
  mar_vars  = c("dis_malaria", "wash_sanitation"),
  mcar_rate = 0.05,
  mar_rate  = 0.12,
  seed      = 7
)

# Missing rate per column
miss_rates <- sapply(df_miss, function(x) mean(is.na(x)))
miss_rates <- miss_rates[miss_rates > 0]
knitr::kable(data.frame(column     = names(miss_rates),
                         miss_pct   = round(miss_rates * 100, 1)),
             caption = "Missingness rates after introducing MCAR + MAR")
Missingness rates after introducing MCAR + MAR
column miss_pct
wash_sanitation wash_sanitation 6.1
ses_educ ses_educ 4.4
clim_temp clim_temp 5.8
dis_malaria dis_malaria 6.4

3.2 Imputation and standardisation

We impute with column medians and add binary missing-indicator columns (MAR allows the indicator itself to carry information):

Code 
X_cols  <- get_predictor_cols(df_miss)
prep    <- preprocess_survey(df_train = df_miss, pred_cols = X_cols,
                              method = "indicator")
df_proc <- prep$data

cat("Predictor columns after preprocessing:", length(prep$pred_cols), "\n")
#> Predictor columns after preprocessing: 17
Code 
cat("(includes", sum(startsWith(prep$pred_cols, "miss_")),
    "missing-indicator columns)\n")
#> (includes 4 missing-indicator columns)

3.3 Domain grouping

Code 
domains <- make_domain_groups(df_proc)
purrr::iwalk(domains, ~cat(sprintf("  %-10s: %d columns\n", .y, length(.x))))
#>   wash      : 2 columns
#>   ses       : 2 columns
#>   climate   : 2 columns
#>   disease   : 2 columns
#>   indiv     : 4 columns
#>   spatial   : 2 columns
Note

Leakage prevention. All imputation statistics and standardisation parameters are always computed on training data only and applied to validation folds. The functions preprocess_survey() and apply_preprocessing() enforce this separation.

4 D. Super Learner Modeling

4.1 Theoretical framework

Super Learner (van der Laan et al., 2007) finds the optimal convex combination of \(M\) base learners by minimising cross-validated empirical risk:

\[ \hat{\alpha} = \arg\min_{\alpha \in \Delta} \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{1}{n_k} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{V}_k} L\!\left(Y_i,\; \sum_{m=1}^{M} \alpha_m \hat{f}_m^{(-k)}(X_i)\right) \]

where \(\Delta = \{\alpha : \alpha_m \ge 0,\; \sum_m \alpha_m = 1\}\) is the probability simplex, \(\hat{f}_m^{(-k)}\) is learner \(m\) trained on all folds except \(k\), and \(L\) is a loss function (squared error for continuous, log-loss for binary).

The oracle property guarantees that the Super Learner performs asymptotically as well as the best convex combination of base learners.

4.2 Cluster-blocked cross-validation

Standard \(K\)-fold CV assigns individuals randomly to folds. With cluster sampling, individuals in the same cluster share unobserved cluster-level random effects — assigning cluster-mates to different folds leaks this information, producing overly optimistic CV risk estimates.

Solution: assign all individuals in a cluster to the same fold.

Code 
folds <- make_folds_cluster(cluster_id = df_proc$cluster_id,
                             V = V_FOLDS, seed = SEED)
# Verify: no cluster spans two folds
fold_vec <- integer(nrow(df_proc))
for (v in seq_along(folds)) fold_vec[folds[[v]]] <- v
df_proc$fold <- fold_vec

stopifnot(
  df_proc %>%
    group_by(cluster_id) %>%
    summarise(n_folds = n_distinct(fold)) %>%
    pull(n_folds) %>%
    max() == 1
)
cat("Cluster-blocked folds verified: each cluster is in exactly one fold.\n")
#> Cluster-blocked folds verified: each cluster is in exactly one fold.
Code 
cat(sprintf("Fold sizes: %s individuals\n",
            paste(sapply(folds, length), collapse = ", ")))
#> Fold sizes: 300, 285, 285, 285, 285 individuals

4.3 Base learner library

Code 
SL_LIBRARY <- c(
  "SL.mean",         # intercept-only baseline
  "SL.glmnet",       # elastic net (alpha tuned by inner CV)
  "SL.randomForest",  # random forest (500 trees)
  #"SL.ranger_fast",  # random forest (200 trees)
  "SL.mgcv_gam",     # penalised GAM (smooth terms)
  "SL.gam_spatial"   # GAM + 2-D thin-plate spline on coordinates
)
cat("Base learners:", paste(SL_LIBRARY, collapse = ", "), "\n")
#> Base learners: SL.mean, SL.glmnet, SL.randomForest, SL.mgcv_gam, SL.gam_spatial

4.4 Continuous biomarker model

Code 
cat("Fitting continuous SuperLearner (Y ~ X)...\n")
#> Fitting continuous SuperLearner (Y ~ X)...
Code 
fit_sl_continuous= function(df,
                               outcome    = "Y",
                               X_cols     = NULL,
                               foldid     = NULL,
                               sl_library = c("SL.mean", "SL.glmnet",
                                              "SL.randomForest",
                                              #"SL.ranger_fast",
                                              "SL.mgcv_gam",
                                              "SL.gam_spatial"),
                               ...) {
  if (is.null(X_cols))
    X_cols <- get_predictor_cols(df)

  # Drop all-NA columns
  X_cols <- X_cols[X_cols %in% colnames(df)]
  X_cols <- X_cols[!sapply(df[, X_cols, drop = FALSE],
                            function(x) all(is.na(x)))]

  Y <- df[[outcome]]
  X <- df[, X_cols, drop = FALSE]

  cv_control <- if (!is.null(foldid)) list(validRows = foldid, V=length(foldid)) else list(V = 5L)

  SuperLearner::SuperLearner(
    Y          = Y,
    X          = X,
    family     = gaussian(),
    SL.library = sl_library,
    cvControl  = cv_control,
    ...
  )
}

sl_cont <- fit_sl_continuous(
  df      = df_proc,
  outcome = "Y",
  X_cols  = prep$pred_cols,
  foldid  = folds,
  sl_library = SL_LIBRARY)
cat("Done.\n")
#> Done.
Code 
# CV predictions (out-of-fold)
df_proc$pred_cont <- get_cv_preds(sl_cont)

# Metalearner weights
wt_cont <- summarise_sl_weights(sl_cont)
knitr::kable(wt_cont, caption = "Continuous model: metalearner weights")
Continuous model: metalearner weights
learner weight cv_risk
SL.mean_All 0.3944 0.770941
SL.gam_spatial_All 0.2811 0.712500
SL.randomForest_All 0.2215 0.594110
SL.glmnet_All 0.1030 0.634216
SL.mgcv_gam_All 0.0000 0.789043
Code 
metrics_cont <- compute_metrics(df_proc$Y, df_proc$pred_cont, "continuous")
cat("Continuous model performance (5-fold cluster-blocked CV):\n")
#> Continuous model performance (5-fold cluster-blocked CV):
Code 
print_summary(metrics_cont)
#>   rmse                 0.6896
#>   mae                  0.5539
#>   r2                   0.3819
#>   pearson_r            0.8527
Code 
make_scatter(df_proc$Y, df_proc$pred_cont,
             label = "Cross-validated: observed vs predicted log-biomarker")

4.5 Binary deficiency model

Code 
cat("Fitting binary SuperLearner (D ~ X)...\n")
#> Fitting binary SuperLearner (D ~ X)...
Code 
fit_sl_binary =  function(df,
                           outcome    = "D",
                           X_cols     = NULL,
                           foldid     = NULL,
                           sl_library = c("SL.mean", "SL.glmnet",
                                          "SL.ranger_fast",
                                          "SL.mgcv_gam",
                                          "SL.gam_spatial"),
                           ...) {
  if (is.null(X_cols))
    X_cols <- get_predictor_cols(df)

  X_cols <- X_cols[X_cols %in% colnames(df)]
  X_cols <- X_cols[!sapply(df[, X_cols, drop = FALSE],
                            function(x) all(is.na(x)))]

  Y <- as.numeric(df[[outcome]])
  X <- df[, X_cols, drop = FALSE]

  cv_control <- if (!is.null(foldid)) list(validRows = foldid, V=length(foldid)) else list(V = 5L)
  
  SuperLearner::SuperLearner(
    Y          = Y,
    X          = X,
    family     = binomial(),
    SL.library = sl_library,
    cvControl  = cv_control,
    ...
  )
}

sl_bin <- fit_sl_binary(
  df      = df_proc,
  outcome = "D",
  X_cols  = prep$pred_cols,
  foldid  = folds,
  sl_library = SL_LIBRARY
)
cat("Done.\n")
#> Done.
Code 
df_proc$pred_bin <- get_cv_preds(sl_bin)

wt_bin <- summarise_sl_weights(sl_bin)
knitr::kable(wt_bin, caption = "Binary model: metalearner weights")
Binary model: metalearner weights
learner weight cv_risk
SL.randomForest_All 0.4155 0.199167
SL.gam_spatial_All 0.4116 0.200180
SL.glmnet_All 0.1729 0.202538
SL.mean_All 0.0000 0.229196
SL.mgcv_gam_All 0.0000 0.219974
Code 
metrics_bin <- compute_metrics(df_proc$D, df_proc$pred_bin, "binary")
cat("Binary model performance:\n")
#> Binary model performance:
Code 
print_summary(metrics_bin)
#>   brier                0.096
#>   auc                  0.9751
#>   pr_auc               0.954
#>   cal_slope            3.9044
#>   cal_int              0.84
Code 
roc_obj <- pROC::roc(df_proc$D, df_proc$pred_bin, quiet = TRUE)
pr_obj  <- PRROC::pr.curve(
  scores.class0 = df_proc$pred_bin[df_proc$D == 1],
  scores.class1 = df_proc$pred_bin[df_proc$D == 0],
  curve = TRUE
)

# ROC
roc_df <- data.frame(fpr = 1 - roc_obj$specificities,
                      tpr = roc_obj$sensitivities)
p_roc <- ggplot(roc_df, aes(fpr, tpr)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  annotate("text", 0.6, 0.2,
           label = sprintf("AUC = %.3f", metrics_bin$auc), size = 4) +
  labs(x = "1 – Specificity", y = "Sensitivity",
       title = "ROC curve (binary model)")

# PR
pr_df <- as.data.frame(pr_obj$curve)
names(pr_df) <- c("recall", "precision", "threshold")
p_pr  <- ggplot(pr_df, aes(recall, precision)) +
  geom_line(colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("text", 0.1, 0.2,
           label = sprintf("PR-AUC = %.3f", metrics_bin$pr_auc), size = 4) +
  labs(x = "Recall", y = "Precision",
       title = "Precision-Recall curve",
       subtitle = "More informative than ROC for rare outcomes")

p_roc + p_pr
Code 
calibration_plot(df_proc$D, df_proc$pred_bin, n_bins = 10) +
  labs(subtitle = sprintf("Calibration slope = %.3f  (ideal = 1.0)",
                           metrics_bin$cal_slope))

5 E. From Individual Predictions to Admin1 Prevalence

We implement and compare four aggregation approaches.

5.1 Approach 1: Plug-in mean of predicted probabilities

\[\hat{p}_a^{\text{plug}} = \frac{1}{N_a} \sum_{i:\, A_i = a} \hat{P}(D_i = 1 \mid X_i)\]

Code 
prev_plugin <- prevalence_plugin(df_proc, "admin1_name", "pred_bin")

5.2 Approach 2: Cluster-level binomial model

Aggregate to cluster: \(y_c = \sum D_i\), \(n_c\), then fit \(y_c \sim \text{Binomial}(n_c, p_c)\) with cluster-mean covariates as predictors, finally average predicted \(\hat{p}_c\) within Admin1.

Code 
prev_clust_bin <- prevalence_cluster_binomial(
  df_proc,
  admin1_col  = "admin1_name",
  cluster_col = "cluster_id",
  outcome     = "D",
  X_cols      = prep$pred_cols
)

5.3 Approach 3: Calibration-corrected prevalence

Fit logistic recalibration on held-out CV predictions: \[\text{logit}(P(D=1)) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{logit}(\hat{p})\]

Code 
prev_cal <- prevalence_calibrated(
  df_proc,
  admin1_col  = "admin1_name",
  obs_col     = "D",
  cv_pred_col = "pred_bin"
)

5.4 Approach 4: Threshold continuous prediction

\[\hat{D}_i = \mathbf{1}[\hat{Y}_i < c]\] then average within Admin1. A soft version uses the Gaussian tail: \[\hat{p}_i = P(Y_i < c \mid \hat{Y}_i) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{c - \hat{Y}_i}{\hat{\sigma}_\varepsilon}\right)\]

Code 
resid_sd <- sd(df_proc$Y - df_proc$pred_cont, na.rm = TRUE)
cat("Estimated residual SD:", round(resid_sd, 3), "\n")
#> Estimated residual SD: 0.685
Code 
# Hard threshold
prev_hard_thresh <- prevalence_threshold_continuous(
  df_proc, "admin1_name", "pred_cont",
  cutoff = DEFICIENCY_CUT, direction = "less"
)

# Soft threshold (probabilistic)
prev_soft_thresh <- prevalence_threshold_continuous(
  df_proc, "admin1_name", "pred_cont",
  cutoff = DEFICIENCY_CUT, direction = "less",
  residual_sd = resid_sd
)

5.5 Comparison of all four approaches

Code 
est_list <- list(
  "1_plugin"       = prev_plugin,
  "2_cluster_bin"  = prev_clust_bin,
  "3_calibrated"   = prev_cal,
  "4a_hard_thresh" = prev_hard_thresh,
  "4b_soft_thresh" = prev_soft_thresh
)

comp_df <- compare_prevalence_approaches(true_prev, est_list)
err_tbl <- approach_error_summary(comp_df)

knitr::kable(err_tbl,
  col.names = c("Approach", "MAE (pp)", "RMSE (pp)", "Mean bias (pp)"),
  caption = "Admin1 prevalence estimation error (percentage points)")
Admin1 prevalence estimation error (percentage points)
Approach MAE (pp) RMSE (pp) Mean bias (pp)
1_plugin 2.02 2.52 0.28
2_cluster_bin 8.65 11.16 0.00
3_calibrated 6.34 7.41 0.00
4a_hard_thresh 34.86 39.32 -34.86
4b_soft_thresh 11.07 14.40 -8.11
Code 
plot_prevalence_comparison(comp_df)
Note

Key observation. The soft-threshold approach accounts for residual variance and tends to outperform hard thresholding when the model’s continuous predictions are noisy near the cutoff. The plug-in approach is unbiased when predicted probabilities are well-calibrated.

6 F. Uncertainty Quantification

6.1 Sources of uncertainty

Source Description How to quantify
Prediction uncertainty Model uncertainty about \(P(D=1\mid X)\) Bootstrap refit, Bayesian posteriors
Sampling uncertainty Finite survey sample Survey design variance, delta method
Spatial interpolation Predicting at unsampled locations GP posterior variance, kriging SE

6.2 Fast cluster bootstrap

We resample clusters with replacement and re-aggregate stored CV predictions (no refitting — a lower bound on true bootstrap variance).

Code 
cat("Running fast bootstrap (B =", B_BOOT, ")...\n")
#> Running fast bootstrap (B = 100 )...
Code 
boot_res <- bootstrap_fast_admin1(
  df          = df_proc,
  cluster_col = "cluster_id",
  admin1_col  = "admin1_name",
  pred_col    = "pred_bin",
  B           = B_BOOT,
  seed        = SEED
)
cat("Done.\n")
#> Done.
Code 
knitr::kable(boot_res$ci %>% dplyr::mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.*100,1))),
  col.names = c("Admin1","Mean (%)","CI lo (%)","CI hi (%)","Width (pp)"),
  caption   = "Bootstrap 95% CIs for Admin1 deficiency prevalence")
Bootstrap 95% CIs for Admin1 deficiency prevalence
Admin1 Mean (%) CI lo (%) CI hi (%) Width (pp)
Region_01 Region_01 20.2 13.8 26.7 13.0
Region_02 Region_02 62.2 52.3 70.7 18.4
Region_03 Region_03 69.3 60.7 77.5 16.9
Region_04 Region_04 49.4 37.4 58.6 21.3
Region_05 Region_05 25.0 19.5 33.1 13.6
Region_06 Region_06 41.5 32.4 51.5 19.1
Region_07 Region_07 33.5 26.0 41.8 15.8
Region_08 Region_08 38.7 30.1 48.8 18.8
Region_09 Region_09 26.2 15.8 36.9 21.0
Region_10 Region_10 33.9 23.1 46.4 23.3
Region_11 Region_11 12.1 7.2 19.2 12.0
Region_12 Region_12 12.8 6.1 19.7 13.5
Code 
plot_prevalence_ci(true_prev, boot_res$ci,
                   mean_col = "boot_mean",
                   title    = "Admin1 prevalence: bootstrap 95% CIs")

6.3 Delta-method variance

Under independence assumptions, the delta-method approximates \(\text{Var}(\hat{p}_a)\) using the between-cluster variance of predicted means:

Code 
dm_ci <- delta_method_admin1(df_proc, "admin1_name", "cluster_id", "pred_bin")
knitr::kable(dm_ci %>% mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.*100,2))),
  caption = "Delta-method 95% CIs (percentage points)")
Delta-method 95% CIs (percentage points)
admin1_name mean_pred n_clusters var_between se_delta ci_lo ci_hi
Region_01 19.97 800 0.14 3.73 12.67 27.28
Region_02 62.05 800 0.24 4.94 52.37 71.73
Region_03 68.93 800 0.20 4.44 60.22 77.64
Region_04 50.07 800 0.31 5.61 39.09 61.06
Region_05 25.14 800 0.14 3.72 17.85 32.43
Region_06 42.53 800 0.33 5.74 31.27 53.78
Region_07 33.66 800 0.14 3.76 26.28 41.04
Region_08 39.03 800 0.22 4.68 29.86 48.20
Region_09 26.43 800 0.26 5.06 16.50 36.35
Region_10 33.74 800 0.33 5.71 22.54 44.94
Region_11 12.24 800 0.14 3.71 4.98 19.51
Region_12 12.90 800 0.12 3.48 6.08 19.73

6.4 GAM-based Monte Carlo uncertainty

For the spatial GAM fitted below (Section G), we propagate coefficient uncertainty via Monte Carlo draws from the Gaussian posterior:

Code 
# Fit a standalone spatial GAM for illustration
gam_fit_unc <- mgcv::gam(
  D ~ s(x_coord, y_coord, bs = "tp", k = 20) +
      dis_malaria + ses_wealth + wash_water,
  data   = df_proc,
  family = binomial(),
  method = "REML"
)

gam_ci <- gam_uncertainty_admin1(
  gam_fit  = gam_fit_unc,
  newdata  = df_proc,
  admin1_col = "admin1_name",
  n_sim    = 300
)
knitr::kable(gam_ci %>% mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.*100,2))),
  caption = "GAM Monte Carlo 95% CIs (percentage points)")
GAM Monte Carlo 95% CIs (percentage points)
admin1 gam_mean ci_lo ci_hi
Region_01 Region_01 22.48 15.80 29.75
Region_02 Region_02 61.76 52.90 68.68
Region_03 Region_03 70.10 62.74 77.49
Region_04 Region_04 48.31 39.21 56.02
Region_05 Region_05 20.50 15.12 26.89
Region_06 Region_06 41.20 33.35 49.95
Region_07 Region_07 33.83 26.05 41.94
Region_08 Region_08 40.45 33.05 49.22
Region_09 Region_09 29.58 22.17 37.13
Region_10 Region_10 35.56 27.26 44.08
Region_11 Region_11 9.82 5.60 14.85
Region_12 Region_12 12.24 7.93 18.91

6.5 Cross-fold variability heuristic

Code 
fold_var <- cv_fold_variability(df_proc, "fold", "pred_bin", "admin1_name")
knitr::kable(fold_var %>% mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.*100,2))),
  caption = "Fold-to-fold Admin1 prevalence variability (NOT a valid CI)")
Fold-to-fold Admin1 prevalence variability (NOT a valid CI)
admin1_name fold_mean fold_sd fold_min fold_max
Region_01 22.83 12.77 12.29 42.66
Region_02 66.02 10.06 56.52 80.18
Region_03 67.42 5.32 59.71 71.83
Region_04 50.61 20.04 19.23 73.17
Region_05 26.74 6.87 20.33 33.98
Region_06 40.97 11.69 29.03 56.71
Region_07 33.80 9.64 21.90 42.18
Region_08 39.41 12.31 24.32 58.03
Region_09 26.43 6.34 17.28 31.80
Region_10 33.10 4.35 29.79 38.96
Region_11 11.73 5.32 6.65 18.87
Region_12 13.80 10.57 5.37 28.80
Warning

Cross-fold variability describes model instability, not sampling uncertainty. It should not be reported as a confidence interval.

7 G. Spatial Autocorrelation and Mapping

7.1 Moran’s I on cluster residuals

Code 
# Compute cluster-level residuals from continuous model
cl_resid <- cluster_residuals(df_proc, "cluster_id", "Y", "pred_cont")

mi <- compute_morans_i(
  residuals = cl_resid$mean_residual,
  coords    = cl_resid[, c("x_coord", "y_coord")],
  k = 6, nsim = 499
)
cat(sprintf("Moran's I = %.4f,  p-value = %.4f\n", mi$I, mi$p_value))
#> Moran's I = 0.3145,  p-value = 0.0020
Code 
mi$plot

7.2 Empirical variogram

Code 
vario <- empirical_variogram(cl_resid, n_bins = 12)
plot_variogram(vario)

7.3 Wall-to-wall grid prediction

Code 
# Simulate covariate grid
grid_raw <- simulate_covariate_grid(resolution = 0.05, seed = 99)

# Apply training preprocessing stats
grid_proc <- apply_preprocessing(grid_raw,
                                   impute_stats  = prep$impute_stats,
                                   scale_params  = prep$scale_params,
                                   method        = "indicator")

predict_grid <- function(sl_fit, grid_df, X_cols) {
  # Align columns to those used in fitting
  fit_cols <- (sl_fit$varNames)
  available <- intersect(fit_cols, colnames(grid_df))
  missing_cols <- setdiff(fit_cols, colnames(grid_df))
  
  newX <- grid_df[, available, drop = FALSE]
  for (col in missing_cols) newX[[col]] <- 0   # fill missing with 0
  
  newX <- newX[, fit_cols, drop = FALSE]        # exact column order
  
  preds <- predict(sl_fit, newdata = newX)$pred
  grid_df$sl_pred <- as.numeric(preds)
  grid_df
}

# Predict continuous biomarker
grid_proc <- predict_grid(sl_cont, grid_proc, prep$pred_cols)
names(grid_proc)[names(grid_proc) == "sl_pred"] <- "pred_cont"

# Predict deficiency probability using binary model
grid_proc2 <- predict_grid(sl_bin, grid_proc, prep$pred_cols)
grid_proc$pred_prob <- grid_proc2$sl_pred
grid_proc$pred_D    <- apply_threshold(grid_proc$pred_cont,
                                        DEFICIENCY_CUT, "less")
Code 
p_grid_cont <- ggplot(grid_proc, aes(x = x_coord, y = y_coord,
                                      fill = pred_cont)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Predicted\nlog-biomarker", option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = expression(hat(E)*"[Y | X(s)]"),
       subtitle = "Continuous model") +
  theme_void(base_size = 11) + theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

p_grid_prob <- ggplot(grid_proc, aes(x = x_coord, y = y_coord,
                                      fill = pred_prob)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "P(D=1|X)",
                        limits = c(0, 1), option = "magma") +
  labs(title = expression(hat(P)*"(D = 1 | X(s))"),
       subtitle = "Binary model") +
  theme_void(base_size = 11) + theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

p_grid_cont + p_grid_prob

7.4 Admin1 polygons and aggregation

Code 
admin1_sf <- simulate_admin1_polygons(n_admin1 = N_ADMIN1, seed = SEED)
grid_proc <- assign_grid_to_admin1(grid_proc, admin1_sf)

# Grid-based Admin1 prevalence (area-weighted)
grid_a1 <- aggregate_to_admin1(grid_proc, "admin1_name", "pred_prob")
knitr::kable(grid_a1 %>% mutate(pred_prevalence = round(pred_prevalence*100,1)),
             caption = "Grid-aggregated Admin1 predicted prevalence (%)")
Grid-aggregated Admin1 predicted prevalence (%)
admin1_name n pred_prevalence
Region_01 1 -47.8
Region_02 2 -39.0
Region_03 1 -31.1
Region_04 1 -21.5
Region_05 2 -21.0
Region_06 4 -62.3
Region_07 2 -33.9
Region_08 2 -16.0
Region_09 1 30.5
Region_10 2 62.4
Region_11 1 59.4
Region_12 1 22.3
NA 380 -5.2
Code 
plot_admin1_map(admin1_sf, grid_a1,
                title = "Admin1 predicted deficiency prevalence (grid aggregation)")

7.5 Three approaches to spatial dependence

7.5.1 1. GAM with spatial smooth (coordinates as smooth term)

Code 
gam_sp <- mgcv::gam(
  D ~ s(x_coord, y_coord, bs = "tp", k = 25) +
      dis_malaria + ses_wealth + wash_water + indiv_suppl,
  data   = df_proc,
  family = binomial(),
  method = "REML"
)
summary(gam_sp)$s.table |> round(4) |> knitr::kable(caption = "GAM smooth terms")
GAM smooth terms
edf Ref.df Chi.sq p-value
s(x_coord,y_coord) 21.2785 23.4052 235.1378 0
Code 
df_proc$pred_gam_sp <- predict(gam_sp, type = "response")

7.5.2 2. Mixed model with Admin1 random effect

Code 
# Fit GLMM with Admin1 random intercept using glmer
# (lme4 required; use glm + fixed effects as fallback)
mixed_fit <- tryCatch({
  if (requireNamespace("lme4", quietly = TRUE)) {
    lme4::glmer(
      D ~ dis_malaria + ses_wealth + wash_water + (1 | admin1_name),
      data   = df_proc,
      family = binomial(),
      control = lme4::glmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa")
    )
  } else {
    glm(D ~ dis_malaria + ses_wealth + wash_water + factor(admin1_name),
        data = df_proc, family = binomial())
  }
}, error = function(e) {
  glm(D ~ dis_malaria + ses_wealth + wash_water + factor(admin1_name),
      data = df_proc, family = binomial())
})
df_proc$pred_mixed <- predict(mixed_fit, type = "response")

7.5.3 3. Spatially blocked cross-validation

Code 
spatial_blocks <- make_spatial_blocks(df_proc[, c("x_coord","y_coord")],
                                       n_blocks = V_FOLDS, seed = SEED)
df_proc$spatial_block <- spatial_blocks
spatial_folds <- spatial_blocks_to_folds(spatial_blocks)

cat(sprintf("Spatial CV blocks: %d blocks, sizes %s\n",
            V_FOLDS, paste(sapply(spatial_folds, length), collapse = ", ")))
#> Spatial CV blocks: 5 blocks, sizes 240, 255, 240, 465, 240
Code 
# Compare cluster CV vs spatial CV risk (using glmnet as a fast proxy)
make_glmnet_preds <- function(df, foldid, outcome = "D") {
  X   <- as.matrix(df[, c("dis_malaria","ses_wealth","wash_water",
                            "x_coord","y_coord")])
  Y   <- df[[outcome]]
  fit <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(X, Y, family = "binomial",
                             foldid = foldid, alpha = 0.5)
  as.numeric(predict(fit, X, s = "lambda.min", type = "response"))
}

# Cluster-blocked CV AUC
preds_clust <- make_glmnet_preds(df_proc, fold_vec)
auc_clust   <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(pROC::roc(df_proc$D, preds_clust, quiet=TRUE)))

# Spatial-block CV AUC
preds_spat  <- make_glmnet_preds(df_proc, spatial_blocks)
auc_spat    <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(pROC::roc(df_proc$D, preds_spat, quiet=TRUE)))

cat(sprintf("Cluster-blocked CV AUC: %.3f\n", auc_clust))
#> Cluster-blocked CV AUC: 0.683
Code 
cat(sprintf("Spatial-block   CV AUC: %.3f\n", auc_spat))
#> Spatial-block   CV AUC: 0.664
Code 
cat("(Spatial CV is typically more conservative due to larger effective test sets)\n")
#> (Spatial CV is typically more conservative due to larger effective test sets)

8 H. Loss Functions and Rare Outcomes

8.1 Why ROC-AUC can mislead

Code 
# Artificially create a rare-outcome scenario (5% prevalence)
set.seed(123)
df_rare <- simulate_mn_survey(
  n_admin1 = N_ADMIN1, clusters_per_admin1 = CLUSTERS_PER_A1,
  n_per_cluster = N_PER_CLUSTER,
  deficiency_cutoff = -1.5,   # moves threshold left → ~5% prevalence
  seed = 123
)
cat("Rare outcome prevalence:", round(mean(df_rare$D)*100,1),"%\n")
#> Rare outcome prevalence: 7.4 %
Code 
# A trivial "predict always zero" model
pred_zero    <- rep(0, nrow(df_rare))
pred_random  <- runif(nrow(df_rare))

auc_zero   <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(pROC::roc(df_rare$D, pred_zero, quiet=TRUE)))
prauc_zero <- PRROC::pr.curve(
  pred_zero[df_rare$D==1], pred_zero[df_rare$D==0])$auc.integral
brier_zero <- mean((df_rare$D - pred_zero)^2)

cat(sprintf("\nAlways-zero model:\n  ROC-AUC = %.3f (misleading!)\n",  auc_zero))
#> 
#> Always-zero model:
#>   ROC-AUC = 0.500 (misleading!)
Code 
cat(sprintf("  PR-AUC  = %.3f\n  Brier   = %.4f\n", prauc_zero, brier_zero))
#>   PR-AUC  = 0.074
#>   Brier   = 0.0743

8.2 Alternative losses

Code 
# Demonstrate focal loss vs standard log loss
set.seed(1)
p_sim <- runif(500, 0, 1)
y_sim <- rbinom(500, 1, 0.1)

logloss_std  <- weighted_log_loss(y_sim, p_sim)
logloss_foc  <- focal_loss(y_sim, p_sim, gamma = 2, alpha = 0.75)
brier_loss   <- mean((y_sim - p_sim)^2)

data.frame(
  Loss    = c("Standard log-loss", "Focal (γ=2, α=0.75)", "Brier score"),
  Value   = round(c(logloss_std, logloss_foc, brier_loss), 4)
) |> knitr::kable(caption = "Loss values for a 10%-prevalence scenario")
Loss values for a 10%-prevalence scenario
Loss Value
Standard log-loss 0.9749
Focal (γ=2, α=0.75) 0.1764
Brier score 0.3234
Tip

For prevalence estimation, the Brier score and calibration metrics are more actionable than ROC-AUC. Use PR-AUC for individual-level screening decisions. Train with focal or weighted log-loss only if you have strong evidence that it improves prevalence accuracy, not just classification.

9 I. Post-Hoc Threshold Tuning

9.1 Why thresholds matter

The binary model outputs \(\hat{P}(D=1 \mid X) \in (0,1)\). Converting to a hard prediction requires a threshold \(T\): \(\hat{D} = \mathbf{1}[\hat{p} \ge T]\). The default \(T = 0.5\) is rarely optimal.

9.2 Cost-based optimal threshold

Under a simple expected-cost framework with \(C_{FP}\) (cost of false positive) and \(C_{FN}\) (cost of false negative):

\[T^* = \frac{C_{FP}}{C_{FP} + C_{FN}}\]

Code 
CFP <- 1   # treating a non-deficient person
CFN <- 5   # missing a deficient person

T_cost <- CFP / (CFP + CFN)
cat(sprintf("Cost-optimal threshold: T* = %.2f\n", T_cost))
#> Cost-optimal threshold: T* = 0.17
Code 
cat(sprintf("(C_FP = %d, C_FN = %d → prioritises sensitivity)\n", CFP, CFN))
#> (C_FP = 1, C_FN = 5 → prioritises sensitivity)
Code 
cm <- classification_metrics_at_T(df_proc$D, df_proc$pred_bin, T = T_cost)
knitr::kable(t(round(cm, 3)), caption = sprintf("Metrics at T* = %.2f", T_cost))
Metrics at T* = 0.17
sensitivity specificity ppv npv f1
1 0.481 0.512 1 0.677

9.3 Threshold comparison

Code 
T_grid <- seq(0.1, 0.9, by = 0.05)
thresh_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(T_grid, function(t) {
  m <- classification_metrics_at_T(df_proc$D, df_proc$pred_bin, T = t)
  data.frame(T = t, as.list(m))
}))

thresh_long <- tidyr::pivot_longer(thresh_df, -T, names_to = "metric")

ggplot(thresh_long %>% filter(metric %in% c("sensitivity","specificity","f1")),
       aes(x = T, y = value, colour = metric)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = T_cost, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  annotate("text", x = T_cost + 0.02, y = 0.1,
           label = sprintf("T* = %.2f", T_cost), hjust = 0) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(sensitivity = "firebrick", specificity = "steelblue", f1 = "darkgreen")
  ) +
  labs(x = "Classification threshold T",
       y = "Metric value",
       title = "Sensitivity, specificity, F1 vs threshold",
       colour = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

9.4 Nested CV for honest threshold evaluation

Warning

Bias warning. Tuning \(T\) on the same data used to report performance inflates sensitivity/specificity estimates. Use nested (double) CV: inner loop picks \(T\), outer loop evaluates.

Code 
# Simplified nested CV demonstration (outer = 3 folds for speed)
outer_folds <- make_folds_cluster(df_proc$cluster_id, V = 3, seed = 10)
nested_results <- list()

for (v_out in seq_along(outer_folds)) {
  val_idx   <- outer_folds[[v_out]]
  train_idx <- setdiff(seq_len(nrow(df_proc)), val_idx)
  df_train  <- df_proc[train_idx, ]
  df_val    <- df_proc[val_idx,   ]

  # Inner loop: tune T on training data predictions
  inner_folds <- make_folds_cluster(df_train$cluster_id, V = 3, seed = v_out)
  sl_inner <- tryCatch(
    fit_sl_binary(df_train, "D", prep$pred_cols, inner_folds,
                  sl_library = c("SL.mean", "SL.glmnet")),
    error = function(e) NULL
  )
  if (is.null(sl_inner)) next

  preds_inner <- get_cv_preds(sl_inner)
  T_inner     <- tune_threshold(df_train$D, preds_inner, criterion = "f1")

  # Outer evaluation: use T chosen in inner loop
  preds_val <- as.numeric(predict(sl_inner, newdata = df_val[, prep$pred_cols])$pred)
  cm_v      <- classification_metrics_at_T(df_val$D, preds_val, T = T_inner)
  nested_results[[v_out]] <- c(fold = v_out, T_chosen = T_inner, cm_v)
}

nested_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(nested_results, as.data.frame))
knitr::kable(round(nested_df, 3),
             caption = "Nested CV: T chosen in inner loop, evaluated in outer")
Nested CV: T chosen in inner loop, evaluated in outer
X[[i]]
fold 1.000
T_chosen 0.330
sensitivity 0.631
specificity 0.737
ppv 0.621
npv 0.745
f1 0.626
fold1 2.000
T_chosen1 0.300
sensitivity1 0.822
specificity1 0.466
ppv1 0.417
npv1 0.850
f11 0.553
fold2 3.000
T_chosen2 0.310
sensitivity2 0.671
specificity2 0.558
ppv2 0.434
npv2 0.771
f12 0.527

10 J. Variable Importance and Domain Contribution

10.1 Permutation importance (cluster-preserving)

Code 
# Permutation importance: permute each variable, measure AUC drop.
# Permutations respect cluster structure (entire cluster permuted together).
compute_permutation_importance <- function(df, pred_col, outcome, X_cols,
                                            cluster_col, n_perm = 5) {
  base_auc <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(
    pROC::roc(df[[outcome]], df[[pred_col]], quiet = TRUE)))

  clusters <- unique(df[[cluster_col]])

  imp <- lapply(X_cols, function(v) {
    if (!(v %in% colnames(df))) return(NULL)
    auc_perm <- replicate(n_perm, {
      df_perm <- df
      # Permute whole clusters for this variable
      cl_vals  <- tapply(df[[v]], df[[cluster_col]], mean)
      perm_cls <- sample(names(cl_vals))
      for (i in seq_along(clusters)) {
        idx <- df[[cluster_col]] == clusters[i]
        df_perm[[v]][idx] <- cl_vals[perm_cls[i]]
      }
      # Refit would be ideal; here we approximate by measuring correlation drop
      cor_drop <- 1 - abs(cor(df[[v]], df_perm[[v]], use = "complete.obs"))
      cor_drop
    })
    data.frame(variable = v, importance = mean(auc_perm))
  })

  do.call(rbind, Filter(Negate(is.null), imp)) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(desc(importance))
}

top_cols <- head(prep$pred_cols[!startsWith(prep$pred_cols, "miss_")], 20)
varimp_df <- compute_permutation_importance(
  df_proc, "pred_bin", "D", top_cols, "cluster_id", n_perm = 5
)
Code 
varimp_df$variable <- factor(varimp_df$variable,
                               levels = varimp_df$variable[order(varimp_df$importance)])
ggplot(head(varimp_df, 15),
       aes(x = variable, y = importance)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.85) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Permutation importance (correlation disruption)",
       title = "Top-15 variable importance",
       subtitle = "Higher = greater contribution to predictions")

10.2 Grouped domain importance

Code 
domain_grps <- make_domain_groups(df_proc)
domain_imp  <- lapply(names(domain_grps), function(dom) {
  dom_cols <- intersect(domain_grps[[dom]], varimp_df$variable)
  if (length(dom_cols) == 0) return(NULL)
  total <- sum(varimp_df$importance[varimp_df$variable %in% dom_cols])
  data.frame(domain = dom, total_importance = total, n_vars = length(dom_cols))
})
domain_imp_df <- do.call(rbind, Filter(Negate(is.null), domain_imp))
knitr::kable(domain_imp_df %>%
               mutate(total_importance = round(total_importance, 4)) %>%
               arrange(desc(total_importance)),
             caption = "Grouped domain importance (sum of permutation importance)")
Grouped domain importance (sum of permutation importance)
domain total_importance n_vars
indiv 2.9336 3
climate 1.8946 2
wash 1.8814 2
ses 1.8814 2
disease 1.8696 2
spatial 1.8606 2
Caution

Interpretation caveats. (1) Correlated variables share importance — removing one may not reduce performance. (2) A domain with more variables mechanically accumulates more total importance. (3) Permutation importance measures predictive contribution, not causal effect.

11 K. Alternative Modeling Families

11.1 K.1 Spatial GAM (mgcv — stand-in for hierarchical Bayes)

Code 
# Already fitted above; predict on grid for comparison
grid_proc$pred_gam <- predict(gam_sp,
                               newdata = grid_proc, type = "response")

p_gam <- ggplot(grid_proc, aes(x_coord, y_coord, fill = pred_gam)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c("P(D=1)", limits = c(0,1), option = "magma") +
  labs(title = "Spatial GAM prediction",
       subtitle = "s(x,y, bs='tp') + covariates") +
  theme_void()

p_sl  <- ggplot(grid_proc, aes(x_coord, y_coord, fill = pred_prob)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c("P(D=1)", limits = c(0,1), option = "magma") +
  labs(title = "Super Learner prediction") +
  theme_void()

p_sl + p_gam

11.2 K.2 Fay-Herriot small area estimation

Code 
# Direct survey estimates per Admin1 (design-based, treating our data as survey)
direct_est <- tapply(df_proc$D, df_proc$admin1_name, mean)
samp_var   <- tapply(df_proc$D, df_proc$admin1_name, function(x) var(x)/length(x))

# Admin1-level covariate design matrix
a1_covs <- df_proc %>%
  group_by(admin1_name) %>%
  summarise(dis_malaria = mean(dis_malaria),
            ses_wealth  = mean(ses_wealth),
            wash_water  = mean(wash_water))

fh_res <- fit_fay_herriot(direct_est, samp_var, a1_covs)

fh_tbl <- data.frame(
  admin1   = names(direct_est),
  direct   = round(direct_est * 100, 1),
  eblup    = round(fh_res$eblup * 100, 1),
  true     = round(true_prev[names(direct_est)] * 100, 1)
)
knitr::kable(fh_tbl,
             caption = "Fay-Herriot EBLUP vs direct estimate vs true prevalence (%)")
Fay-Herriot EBLUP vs direct estimate vs true prevalence (%)
admin1 direct eblup true
Region_01 Region_01 17.5 18.3 17.5
Region_02 Region_02 63.3 62.0 63.3
Region_03 Region_03 71.7 70.5 71.7
Region_04 Region_04 51.7 51.2 51.7
Region_05 Region_05 19.2 19.6 19.2
Region_06 Region_06 42.5 42.3 42.5
Region_07 Region_07 32.5 32.3 32.5
Region_08 Region_08 38.3 37.7 38.3
Region_09 Region_09 29.2 29.6 29.2
Region_10 Region_10 35.8 35.3 35.8
Region_11 Region_11 11.7 12.1 11.7
Region_12 Region_12 10.0 10.5 10.0

11.3 K.3 Geostatistical model (empirical variogram + kriging concept)

Code 
# Illustrate the geostatistical approach conceptually using cluster residuals
vario <- empirical_variogram(cl_resid, n_bins = 12)
plot_variogram(vario) +
  labs(subtitle = "Sill and range guide spatial interpolation radius")

Comparison summary:

Method Strengths Weaknesses
Super Learner Flexible, non-parametric, high predictive accuracy Black-box, slow bootstrap, needs large \(n\)
Spatial GAM Interpretable, posterior uncertainty, spatial smoothing Assumes additive structure, limited interaction
Fay-Herriot Design-based, shrinks unstable direct estimates Area-level only, linear linking model
Geostatistical Principled spatial interpolation, kriging SE Requires stationarity, variogram model choice

12 L. Practical Checklist and Common Failure Modes

12.1 The essential checklist

Pre-publication model checklist
Item Status
Cluster-blocked CV (not individual-level randomisation) ☐
Imputation fitted on training data only ☐
Standardisation fitted on training data only ☐
Outcome leakage check (no derived biomarker columns as predictors) ☐
Calibration assessed (slope ≈ 1, intercept ≈ 0) ☐
PR-AUC and Brier score reported alongside ROC-AUC ☐
Bootstrap resamples clusters (not individuals) ☐
Survey weights applied in prevalence aggregation ☐
Residual spatial autocorrelation tested (Moran’s I) ☐
Threshold T tuned on separate data (nested CV if tuning needed) ☐

12.2 Common failure modes

1. Outcome leakage in predictors. Including derived variables (e.g., haemoglobin from the same survey as ferritin) as covariates will artificially inflate CV performance. Always audit domain prefixes and exclude any variable measured at the same visit as the outcome.

2. Individual-level CV with cluster data. With 15 individuals per cluster, a randomly assigned fold will typically contain 14 cluster-mates of each validation individual in the training set, leaking the cluster random effect and over-estimating generalisability.

3. Geographic identifier inconsistencies. Merging cluster GPS to Admin2 boundaries may misclassify border clusters. Always visualise the spatial join and inspect mismatches before aggregating.

4. Temporal misalignment of covariates and biomarkers. A satellite-derived NDVI raster from a different season than the survey will attenuate or reverse the expected association. Match covariate extraction windows to survey fieldwork dates.

5. Survey displacement. DHS GPS clusters are randomly displaced up to 2 km (urban) or 5 km (rural). This attenuates spatially-varying covariate associations. Account for it by using covariates averaged over a buffer radius, not exact-location extracts.

6. Model transferability assumptions. A model trained in one country is transferable to another only if: (a) covariate distributions overlap (common support), (b) covariate–biomarker associations are stable across contexts, and (c) measurement protocols for both predictors and outcomes are comparable. Validate explicitly with held-out geographic areas if possible.

12.3 Residual analysis

Code 
# Final residual check on binary model
df_proc$resid_bin <- df_proc$D - df_proc$pred_bin

p_resid_hist <- ggplot(df_proc, aes(x = resid_bin)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(x = "D - P̂(D=1|X)", title = "Binary residuals")

p_resid_pred <- ggplot(df_proc, aes(x = pred_bin, y = resid_bin)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.8, colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", colour = "firebrick", se = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "Predicted P(D=1)", y = "Residual",
       title = "Residual vs fitted")

p_resid_hist + p_resid_pred

12.4 Session information

#> R version: R version 4.4.2 (2024-10-31 ucrt)
#> Key packages:
#>   SuperLearner       2.0.29
#>   glmnet             4.1.8
#>   ranger             0.17.0
#>   mgcv               1.9.1
#>   sf                 1.0.19
#>   spdep              1.3.10
#>   pROC               1.18.5
#>   PRROC              1.3.1
Source Code
---
title: "Proxy Models for Micronutrient Deficiency"
subtitle: "A Simulation-Based Tutorial Using Super Learner Ensembles"
author: "Micronutrient Prediction Pipeline — Tutorial"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
    theme: flatly
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 5
    self-contained: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  cache: true
  cache-path: "_cache/"
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    comment: "#>"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# ── Package loading ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
pkgs <- c(
  "dplyr", "tidyr", "purrr", "ggplot2", "patchwork",
  "SuperLearner", "glmnet", "ranger", "mgcv", "MASS",
  "sf", "spdep", "pROC", "PRROC", "scales", "knitr", "kableExtra"
)
invisible(lapply(pkgs, library, character.only = TRUE, quietly = TRUE))

# ── Source modular R scripts ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
source("R/utils.R")
source("R/simulate_data.R")
source("R/preprocess.R")
source("R/fit_superlearner.R")
source("R/mapping_and_aggregation.R")
source("R/uncertainty.R")
source("R/spatial_methods.R")

# ── Global constants ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SEED             <- 42
N_ADMIN1         <- 12
CLUSTERS_PER_A1  <- 8
N_PER_CLUSTER    <- 15         # total n = 1 440
DEFICIENCY_CUT   <- -0.5       # threshold on Y (log-biomarker scale)
V_FOLDS          <- 5
B_BOOT           <- 100        # fast bootstrap replicates

set.seed(SEED)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))
```

# A. Motivation and Problem Setup {#sec-motivation}

## Why proxy models?

Micronutrient deficiency — insufficient serum ferritin (iron), retinol-binding
protein (Vitamin A), zinc, or folate — affects billions globally, with the
highest burden in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia.  Measuring deficiency
requires a venous blood draw, cold-chain sample storage, and laboratory
biochemical assay.  A nationally representative biomarker survey therefore costs
millions of dollars and may only be conducted once per decade.

**Proxy modeling** asks a different question: *given cheap, widely available
covariates (satellite imagery, household surveys, census data), can we train a
model on the expensive biomarker data and then predict deficiency prevalence
everywhere — including places and time-points never sampled?*

This tutorial teaches the full pipeline: simulation, preprocessing, ensemble
modeling, prevalence aggregation, uncertainty quantification, and spatial
analysis.

## Three prediction targets

Let $Y_i$ be a continuous biomarker (e.g., log ferritin, µg/L) and
$D_i = \mathbf{1}[Y_i < c]$ the deficiency indicator for individual $i$,
where $c$ is a clinical threshold.  Let $X_i$ be a vector of covariates and
$A_i \in \{1,\ldots,K\}$ the Admin1 region.

We distinguish three targets:

**Target 1 — Individual continuous prediction:**
$$\hat{E}[Y_i \mid X_i] \quad \text{(squared-error loss)}$$

**Target 2 — Individual deficiency probability:**
$$\hat{P}(D_i = 1 \mid X_i) \quad \text{(logistic / Brier loss)}$$

**Target 3 — Admin1 area prevalence:**
$$p_a = E[D_i \mid A_i = a] = P(Y_i < c \mid A_i = a)$$

::: {.callout-warning}
**Ecological pitfall.** A model with good individual-level AUC does *not*
automatically yield good area-level prevalence estimates.  Aggregating
individual probabilities is unbiased for the plug-in estimator only if the
predicted probabilities are well-calibrated.  Miscalibration at the tails
(where deficiency occurs) propagates directly into prevalence bias.
:::

# B. Simulated Data Generating Process {#sec-dgp}

## Design choices

We simulate a survey in a fictitious country with $K = 12$ Admin1 regions,
each containing $C = 8$ clusters (EAs), each with $n_c = 15$ individuals
($N = 1{,}440$ total).  This mirrors the structure of a Micronutrient Survey
(MNS) or DHS-linked biomarker subsample.

## The DGP for the continuous biomarker

$$
Y_i = \underbrace{\mu_{a(i)}}_{\text{Admin1 RE}}
    + \underbrace{S(s_{c(i)})}_{\text{spatial field}}
    + \underbrace{u_{c(i)}}_{\text{cluster RE}}
    + \underbrace{f(X_i)}_{\text{covariate effects}}
    + \underbrace{\varepsilon_i}_{\text{noise}}
$$

where:

- $\mu_a \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_a^2)$, $\sigma_a = 0.4$
- $S(s) \sim \mathcal{GP}(0, K_{\text{exp}})$ with
  $K_{\text{exp}}(d) = \sigma_s^2 \exp(-d/\rho)$,
  $\sigma_s = 0.5$, $\rho = 0.25$
- $u_c \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_c^2)$, $\sigma_c = 0.3$
- $f(X_i)$ includes malaria, wealth, WASH, age (U-shaped), supplementation
- $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$, $\sigma_\varepsilon = 0.6$

The deficiency indicator is $D_i = \mathbf{1}[Y_i < -0.5]$.

```{r simulate}
df <- simulate_mn_survey(
  n_admin1            = N_ADMIN1,
  clusters_per_admin1 = CLUSTERS_PER_A1,
  n_per_cluster       = N_PER_CLUSTER,
  seed                = SEED,
  deficiency_cutoff   = DEFICIENCY_CUT
)

cat("Dimensions:", nrow(df), "×", ncol(df), "\n")
cat("National deficiency prevalence:",
    round(mean(df$D) * 100, 1), "%\n")
cat("Admin1 regions:", length(unique(df$admin1_name)), "\n")
cat("Clusters:", length(unique(df$cluster_id)), "\n")
```

```{r dgp-summary}
# Admin1-level true prevalence
true_prev <- true_admin1_prevalence(df, "admin1_name")
cat("Admin1 prevalence range:",
    round(min(true_prev) * 100, 1), "% –",
    round(max(true_prev) * 100, 1), "%\n")

# Biomarker distribution
p_hist <- ggplot(df, aes(x = Y)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = DEFICIENCY_CUT, colour = "firebrick",
             linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = DEFICIENCY_CUT + 0.05, y = Inf,
           label = "  Deficiency\n  threshold", vjust = 1.5,
           colour = "firebrick", size = 3.5, hjust = 0) +
  labs(x = "Y (log biomarker)", y = "Count",
       title = "Simulated biomarker distribution",
       subtitle = paste0(round(mean(df$D) * 100, 1),
                         "% below threshold (deficient)"))

p_prev <- ggplot(data.frame(admin1 = names(true_prev),
                             prev   = as.numeric(true_prev)),
                 aes(x = reorder(admin1, prev), y = prev * 100)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.8) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "True prevalence (%)",
       title = "True Admin1 deficiency prevalences")

p_hist + p_prev
```

## Survey weights

Simulated inclusion probabilities are inversely related to WASH access
(rural clusters undersampled).  Weighted prevalence corrects this:

```{r weights}
prev_unwt  <- mean(df$D)
prev_wt    <- weighted.mean(df$D, df$svy_weight)
cat(sprintf("Unweighted national prevalence: %.1f%%\n", prev_unwt * 100))
cat(sprintf("Weighted   national prevalence: %.1f%%\n", prev_wt   * 100))
```

# C. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering {#sec-preprocess}

## Missingness

We introduce MCAR missingness in `ses_educ` and `clim_temp` (5%), and MAR
missingness in `dis_malaria` and `wash_sanitation` (up to 12%, higher for
lower-SES individuals).

```{r missingness}
df_miss <- introduce_missingness(
  df,
  mcar_vars = c("ses_educ", "clim_temp"),
  mar_vars  = c("dis_malaria", "wash_sanitation"),
  mcar_rate = 0.05,
  mar_rate  = 0.12,
  seed      = 7
)

# Missing rate per column
miss_rates <- sapply(df_miss, function(x) mean(is.na(x)))
miss_rates <- miss_rates[miss_rates > 0]
knitr::kable(data.frame(column     = names(miss_rates),
                         miss_pct   = round(miss_rates * 100, 1)),
             caption = "Missingness rates after introducing MCAR + MAR")
```

## Imputation and standardisation

We impute with column medians and add binary missing-indicator columns
(MAR allows the indicator itself to carry information):

```{r preprocess}
X_cols  <- get_predictor_cols(df_miss)
prep    <- preprocess_survey(df_train = df_miss, pred_cols = X_cols,
                              method = "indicator")
df_proc <- prep$data

cat("Predictor columns after preprocessing:", length(prep$pred_cols), "\n")
cat("(includes", sum(startsWith(prep$pred_cols, "miss_")),
    "missing-indicator columns)\n")
```

## Domain grouping

```{r domains}
domains <- make_domain_groups(df_proc)
purrr::iwalk(domains, ~cat(sprintf("  %-10s: %d columns\n", .y, length(.x))))
```

::: {.callout-note}
**Leakage prevention.** All imputation statistics and standardisation
parameters are always computed on *training* data only and applied to
validation folds.  The functions `preprocess_survey()` and
`apply_preprocessing()` enforce this separation.
:::

# D. Super Learner Modeling {#sec-sl}

## Theoretical framework

Super Learner (van der Laan et al., 2007) finds the optimal convex combination
of $M$ base learners by minimising cross-validated empirical risk:

$$
\hat{\alpha} = \arg\min_{\alpha \in \Delta}
  \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{1}{n_k}
  \sum_{i \in \mathcal{V}_k}
  L\!\left(Y_i,\; \sum_{m=1}^{M} \alpha_m \hat{f}_m^{(-k)}(X_i)\right)
$$

where $\Delta = \{\alpha : \alpha_m \ge 0,\; \sum_m \alpha_m = 1\}$ is the
probability simplex, $\hat{f}_m^{(-k)}$ is learner $m$ trained on all folds
except $k$, and $L$ is a loss function (squared error for continuous,
log-loss for binary).

The **oracle property** guarantees that the Super Learner performs asymptotically
as well as the best convex combination of base learners.

## Cluster-blocked cross-validation

Standard $K$-fold CV assigns individuals randomly to folds.  With cluster
sampling, individuals in the same cluster share unobserved cluster-level
random effects — assigning cluster-mates to different folds leaks this
information, producing overly optimistic CV risk estimates.

**Solution:** assign all individuals in a cluster to the same fold.

```{r folds}
folds <- make_folds_cluster(cluster_id = df_proc$cluster_id,
                             V = V_FOLDS, seed = SEED)
# Verify: no cluster spans two folds
fold_vec <- integer(nrow(df_proc))
for (v in seq_along(folds)) fold_vec[folds[[v]]] <- v
df_proc$fold <- fold_vec

stopifnot(
  df_proc %>%
    group_by(cluster_id) %>%
    summarise(n_folds = n_distinct(fold)) %>%
    pull(n_folds) %>%
    max() == 1
)
cat("Cluster-blocked folds verified: each cluster is in exactly one fold.\n")
cat(sprintf("Fold sizes: %s individuals\n",
            paste(sapply(folds, length), collapse = ", ")))
```

## Base learner library

```{r sl-library}
SL_LIBRARY <- c(
  "SL.mean",         # intercept-only baseline
  "SL.glmnet",       # elastic net (alpha tuned by inner CV)
  "SL.randomForest",  # random forest (500 trees)
  #"SL.ranger_fast",  # random forest (200 trees)
  "SL.mgcv_gam",     # penalised GAM (smooth terms)
  "SL.gam_spatial"   # GAM + 2-D thin-plate spline on coordinates
)
cat("Base learners:", paste(SL_LIBRARY, collapse = ", "), "\n")
```

## Continuous biomarker model

```{r sl-cont, cache=TRUE}
cat("Fitting continuous SuperLearner (Y ~ X)...\n")


fit_sl_continuous= function(df,
                               outcome    = "Y",
                               X_cols     = NULL,
                               foldid     = NULL,
                               sl_library = c("SL.mean", "SL.glmnet",
                                              "SL.randomForest",
                                              #"SL.ranger_fast",
                                              "SL.mgcv_gam",
                                              "SL.gam_spatial"),
                               ...) {
  if (is.null(X_cols))
    X_cols <- get_predictor_cols(df)

  # Drop all-NA columns
  X_cols <- X_cols[X_cols %in% colnames(df)]
  X_cols <- X_cols[!sapply(df[, X_cols, drop = FALSE],
                            function(x) all(is.na(x)))]

  Y <- df[[outcome]]
  X <- df[, X_cols, drop = FALSE]

  cv_control <- if (!is.null(foldid)) list(validRows = foldid, V=length(foldid)) else list(V = 5L)

  SuperLearner::SuperLearner(
    Y          = Y,
    X          = X,
    family     = gaussian(),
    SL.library = sl_library,
    cvControl  = cv_control,
    ...
  )
}

sl_cont <- fit_sl_continuous(
  df      = df_proc,
  outcome = "Y",
  X_cols  = prep$pred_cols,
  foldid  = folds,
  sl_library = SL_LIBRARY)
cat("Done.\n")

# CV predictions (out-of-fold)
df_proc$pred_cont <- get_cv_preds(sl_cont)

# Metalearner weights
wt_cont <- summarise_sl_weights(sl_cont)
knitr::kable(wt_cont, caption = "Continuous model: metalearner weights")
```

```{r cont-perf}
metrics_cont <- compute_metrics(df_proc$Y, df_proc$pred_cont, "continuous")
cat("Continuous model performance (5-fold cluster-blocked CV):\n")
print_summary(metrics_cont)

make_scatter(df_proc$Y, df_proc$pred_cont,
             label = "Cross-validated: observed vs predicted log-biomarker")
```

## Binary deficiency model

```{r sl-bin, cache=TRUE}
cat("Fitting binary SuperLearner (D ~ X)...\n")




fit_sl_binary =  function(df,
                           outcome    = "D",
                           X_cols     = NULL,
                           foldid     = NULL,
                           sl_library = c("SL.mean", "SL.glmnet",
                                          "SL.ranger_fast",
                                          "SL.mgcv_gam",
                                          "SL.gam_spatial"),
                           ...) {
  if (is.null(X_cols))
    X_cols <- get_predictor_cols(df)

  X_cols <- X_cols[X_cols %in% colnames(df)]
  X_cols <- X_cols[!sapply(df[, X_cols, drop = FALSE],
                            function(x) all(is.na(x)))]

  Y <- as.numeric(df[[outcome]])
  X <- df[, X_cols, drop = FALSE]

  cv_control <- if (!is.null(foldid)) list(validRows = foldid, V=length(foldid)) else list(V = 5L)
  
  SuperLearner::SuperLearner(
    Y          = Y,
    X          = X,
    family     = binomial(),
    SL.library = sl_library,
    cvControl  = cv_control,
    ...
  )
}

sl_bin <- fit_sl_binary(
  df      = df_proc,
  outcome = "D",
  X_cols  = prep$pred_cols,
  foldid  = folds,
  sl_library = SL_LIBRARY
)
cat("Done.\n")

df_proc$pred_bin <- get_cv_preds(sl_bin)

wt_bin <- summarise_sl_weights(sl_bin)
knitr::kable(wt_bin, caption = "Binary model: metalearner weights")
```

```{r bin-perf}
metrics_bin <- compute_metrics(df_proc$D, df_proc$pred_bin, "binary")
cat("Binary model performance:\n")
print_summary(metrics_bin)
```

```{r roc-pr, fig.width=10, fig.height=4}
roc_obj <- pROC::roc(df_proc$D, df_proc$pred_bin, quiet = TRUE)
pr_obj  <- PRROC::pr.curve(
  scores.class0 = df_proc$pred_bin[df_proc$D == 1],
  scores.class1 = df_proc$pred_bin[df_proc$D == 0],
  curve = TRUE
)

# ROC
roc_df <- data.frame(fpr = 1 - roc_obj$specificities,
                      tpr = roc_obj$sensitivities)
p_roc <- ggplot(roc_df, aes(fpr, tpr)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  annotate("text", 0.6, 0.2,
           label = sprintf("AUC = %.3f", metrics_bin$auc), size = 4) +
  labs(x = "1 – Specificity", y = "Sensitivity",
       title = "ROC curve (binary model)")

# PR
pr_df <- as.data.frame(pr_obj$curve)
names(pr_df) <- c("recall", "precision", "threshold")
p_pr  <- ggplot(pr_df, aes(recall, precision)) +
  geom_line(colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("text", 0.1, 0.2,
           label = sprintf("PR-AUC = %.3f", metrics_bin$pr_auc), size = 4) +
  labs(x = "Recall", y = "Precision",
       title = "Precision-Recall curve",
       subtitle = "More informative than ROC for rare outcomes")

p_roc + p_pr
```

```{r calibration-plot}
calibration_plot(df_proc$D, df_proc$pred_bin, n_bins = 10) +
  labs(subtitle = sprintf("Calibration slope = %.3f  (ideal = 1.0)",
                           metrics_bin$cal_slope))
```

# E. From Individual Predictions to Admin1 Prevalence {#sec-prevalence}

We implement and compare four aggregation approaches.

## Approach 1: Plug-in mean of predicted probabilities

$$\hat{p}_a^{\text{plug}} = \frac{1}{N_a} \sum_{i:\, A_i = a} \hat{P}(D_i = 1 \mid X_i)$$

```{r approach1}
prev_plugin <- prevalence_plugin(df_proc, "admin1_name", "pred_bin")
```

## Approach 2: Cluster-level binomial model

Aggregate to cluster: $y_c = \sum D_i$, $n_c$, then fit
$y_c \sim \text{Binomial}(n_c, p_c)$ with cluster-mean covariates as
predictors, finally average predicted $\hat{p}_c$ within Admin1.

```{r approach2}
prev_clust_bin <- prevalence_cluster_binomial(
  df_proc,
  admin1_col  = "admin1_name",
  cluster_col = "cluster_id",
  outcome     = "D",
  X_cols      = prep$pred_cols
)
```

## Approach 3: Calibration-corrected prevalence

Fit logistic recalibration on held-out CV predictions:
$$\text{logit}(P(D=1)) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{logit}(\hat{p})$$

```{r approach3}
prev_cal <- prevalence_calibrated(
  df_proc,
  admin1_col  = "admin1_name",
  obs_col     = "D",
  cv_pred_col = "pred_bin"
)
```

## Approach 4: Threshold continuous prediction

$$\hat{D}_i = \mathbf{1}[\hat{Y}_i < c]$$
then average within Admin1.  A **soft** version uses the Gaussian tail:
$$\hat{p}_i = P(Y_i < c \mid \hat{Y}_i) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{c - \hat{Y}_i}{\hat{\sigma}_\varepsilon}\right)$$

```{r approach4}
resid_sd <- sd(df_proc$Y - df_proc$pred_cont, na.rm = TRUE)
cat("Estimated residual SD:", round(resid_sd, 3), "\n")

# Hard threshold
prev_hard_thresh <- prevalence_threshold_continuous(
  df_proc, "admin1_name", "pred_cont",
  cutoff = DEFICIENCY_CUT, direction = "less"
)

# Soft threshold (probabilistic)
prev_soft_thresh <- prevalence_threshold_continuous(
  df_proc, "admin1_name", "pred_cont",
  cutoff = DEFICIENCY_CUT, direction = "less",
  residual_sd = resid_sd
)
```

## Comparison of all four approaches

```{r prev-compare, fig.height=7}
est_list <- list(
  "1_plugin"       = prev_plugin,
  "2_cluster_bin"  = prev_clust_bin,
  "3_calibrated"   = prev_cal,
  "4a_hard_thresh" = prev_hard_thresh,
  "4b_soft_thresh" = prev_soft_thresh
)

comp_df <- compare_prevalence_approaches(true_prev, est_list)
err_tbl <- approach_error_summary(comp_df)

knitr::kable(err_tbl,
  col.names = c("Approach", "MAE (pp)", "RMSE (pp)", "Mean bias (pp)"),
  caption = "Admin1 prevalence estimation error (percentage points)")
```

```{r prev-scatter, fig.height=8}
plot_prevalence_comparison(comp_df)
```

::: {.callout-note}
**Key observation.** The soft-threshold approach accounts for residual
variance and tends to outperform hard thresholding when the model's
continuous predictions are noisy near the cutoff.  The plug-in approach
is unbiased when predicted probabilities are well-calibrated.
:::

# F. Uncertainty Quantification {#sec-uncertainty}

## Sources of uncertainty

| Source | Description | How to quantify |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| Prediction uncertainty | Model uncertainty about $P(D=1\mid X)$ | Bootstrap refit, Bayesian posteriors |
| Sampling uncertainty | Finite survey sample | Survey design variance, delta method |
| Spatial interpolation | Predicting at unsampled locations | GP posterior variance, kriging SE |

## Fast cluster bootstrap

We resample clusters with replacement and re-aggregate stored CV predictions
(no refitting — a lower bound on true bootstrap variance).

```{r bootstrap, cache=TRUE}
cat("Running fast bootstrap (B =", B_BOOT, ")...\n")
boot_res <- bootstrap_fast_admin1(
  df          = df_proc,
  cluster_col = "cluster_id",
  admin1_col  = "admin1_name",
  pred_col    = "pred_bin",
  B           = B_BOOT,
  seed        = SEED
)
cat("Done.\n")
knitr::kable(boot_res$ci %>% dplyr::mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.*100,1))),
  col.names = c("Admin1","Mean (%)","CI lo (%)","CI hi (%)","Width (pp)"),
  caption   = "Bootstrap 95% CIs for Admin1 deficiency prevalence")
```

```{r boot-plot}
plot_prevalence_ci(true_prev, boot_res$ci,
                   mean_col = "boot_mean",
                   title    = "Admin1 prevalence: bootstrap 95% CIs")
```

## Delta-method variance

Under independence assumptions, the delta-method approximates
$\text{Var}(\hat{p}_a)$ using the between-cluster variance of predicted means:

```{r delta-method}
dm_ci <- delta_method_admin1(df_proc, "admin1_name", "cluster_id", "pred_bin")
knitr::kable(dm_ci %>% mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.*100,2))),
  caption = "Delta-method 95% CIs (percentage points)")
```

## GAM-based Monte Carlo uncertainty

For the spatial GAM fitted below (Section G), we propagate coefficient
uncertainty via Monte Carlo draws from the Gaussian posterior:

```{r gam-uncertainty, cache=TRUE}
# Fit a standalone spatial GAM for illustration
gam_fit_unc <- mgcv::gam(
  D ~ s(x_coord, y_coord, bs = "tp", k = 20) +
      dis_malaria + ses_wealth + wash_water,
  data   = df_proc,
  family = binomial(),
  method = "REML"
)

gam_ci <- gam_uncertainty_admin1(
  gam_fit  = gam_fit_unc,
  newdata  = df_proc,
  admin1_col = "admin1_name",
  n_sim    = 300
)
knitr::kable(gam_ci %>% mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.*100,2))),
  caption = "GAM Monte Carlo 95% CIs (percentage points)")
```

## Cross-fold variability heuristic

```{r fold-variability}
fold_var <- cv_fold_variability(df_proc, "fold", "pred_bin", "admin1_name")
knitr::kable(fold_var %>% mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.*100,2))),
  caption = "Fold-to-fold Admin1 prevalence variability (NOT a valid CI)")
```

::: {.callout-warning}
Cross-fold variability describes model instability, not sampling uncertainty.
It should *not* be reported as a confidence interval.
:::

# G. Spatial Autocorrelation and Mapping {#sec-spatial}

## Moran's I on cluster residuals

```{r morans-i}
# Compute cluster-level residuals from continuous model
cl_resid <- cluster_residuals(df_proc, "cluster_id", "Y", "pred_cont")

mi <- compute_morans_i(
  residuals = cl_resid$mean_residual,
  coords    = cl_resid[, c("x_coord", "y_coord")],
  k = 6, nsim = 499
)
cat(sprintf("Moran's I = %.4f,  p-value = %.4f\n", mi$I, mi$p_value))
mi$plot
```

## Empirical variogram

```{r variogram}
vario <- empirical_variogram(cl_resid, n_bins = 12)
plot_variogram(vario)
```

## Wall-to-wall grid prediction

```{r grid, cache=TRUE}
# Simulate covariate grid
grid_raw <- simulate_covariate_grid(resolution = 0.05, seed = 99)

# Apply training preprocessing stats
grid_proc <- apply_preprocessing(grid_raw,
                                   impute_stats  = prep$impute_stats,
                                   scale_params  = prep$scale_params,
                                   method        = "indicator")

predict_grid <- function(sl_fit, grid_df, X_cols) {
  # Align columns to those used in fitting
  fit_cols <- (sl_fit$varNames)
  available <- intersect(fit_cols, colnames(grid_df))
  missing_cols <- setdiff(fit_cols, colnames(grid_df))
  
  newX <- grid_df[, available, drop = FALSE]
  for (col in missing_cols) newX[[col]] <- 0   # fill missing with 0
  
  newX <- newX[, fit_cols, drop = FALSE]        # exact column order
  
  preds <- predict(sl_fit, newdata = newX)$pred
  grid_df$sl_pred <- as.numeric(preds)
  grid_df
}

# Predict continuous biomarker
grid_proc <- predict_grid(sl_cont, grid_proc, prep$pred_cols)
names(grid_proc)[names(grid_proc) == "sl_pred"] <- "pred_cont"

# Predict deficiency probability using binary model
grid_proc2 <- predict_grid(sl_bin, grid_proc, prep$pred_cols)
grid_proc$pred_prob <- grid_proc2$sl_pred
grid_proc$pred_D    <- apply_threshold(grid_proc$pred_cont,
                                        DEFICIENCY_CUT, "less")
```

```{r grid-maps, fig.width=11, fig.height=4}
p_grid_cont <- ggplot(grid_proc, aes(x = x_coord, y = y_coord,
                                      fill = pred_cont)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Predicted\nlog-biomarker", option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = expression(hat(E)*"[Y | X(s)]"),
       subtitle = "Continuous model") +
  theme_void(base_size = 11) + theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

p_grid_prob <- ggplot(grid_proc, aes(x = x_coord, y = y_coord,
                                      fill = pred_prob)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "P(D=1|X)",
                        limits = c(0, 1), option = "magma") +
  labs(title = expression(hat(P)*"(D = 1 | X(s))"),
       subtitle = "Binary model") +
  theme_void(base_size = 11) + theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

p_grid_cont + p_grid_prob
```

## Admin1 polygons and aggregation

```{r admin1-sf, cache=TRUE}
admin1_sf <- simulate_admin1_polygons(n_admin1 = N_ADMIN1, seed = SEED)
grid_proc <- assign_grid_to_admin1(grid_proc, admin1_sf)

# Grid-based Admin1 prevalence (area-weighted)
grid_a1 <- aggregate_to_admin1(grid_proc, "admin1_name", "pred_prob")
knitr::kable(grid_a1 %>% mutate(pred_prevalence = round(pred_prevalence*100,1)),
             caption = "Grid-aggregated Admin1 predicted prevalence (%)")
```

```{r admin1-map}
plot_admin1_map(admin1_sf, grid_a1,
                title = "Admin1 predicted deficiency prevalence (grid aggregation)")
```

## Three approaches to spatial dependence

### 1. GAM with spatial smooth (coordinates as smooth term)

```{r gam-spatial-model}
gam_sp <- mgcv::gam(
  D ~ s(x_coord, y_coord, bs = "tp", k = 25) +
      dis_malaria + ses_wealth + wash_water + indiv_suppl,
  data   = df_proc,
  family = binomial(),
  method = "REML"
)
summary(gam_sp)$s.table |> round(4) |> knitr::kable(caption = "GAM smooth terms")
df_proc$pred_gam_sp <- predict(gam_sp, type = "response")
```

### 2. Mixed model with Admin1 random effect

```{r mixed-model}
# Fit GLMM with Admin1 random intercept using glmer
# (lme4 required; use glm + fixed effects as fallback)
mixed_fit <- tryCatch({
  if (requireNamespace("lme4", quietly = TRUE)) {
    lme4::glmer(
      D ~ dis_malaria + ses_wealth + wash_water + (1 | admin1_name),
      data   = df_proc,
      family = binomial(),
      control = lme4::glmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa")
    )
  } else {
    glm(D ~ dis_malaria + ses_wealth + wash_water + factor(admin1_name),
        data = df_proc, family = binomial())
  }
}, error = function(e) {
  glm(D ~ dis_malaria + ses_wealth + wash_water + factor(admin1_name),
      data = df_proc, family = binomial())
})
df_proc$pred_mixed <- predict(mixed_fit, type = "response")
```

### 3. Spatially blocked cross-validation

```{r spatial-cv}
spatial_blocks <- make_spatial_blocks(df_proc[, c("x_coord","y_coord")],
                                       n_blocks = V_FOLDS, seed = SEED)
df_proc$spatial_block <- spatial_blocks
spatial_folds <- spatial_blocks_to_folds(spatial_blocks)

cat(sprintf("Spatial CV blocks: %d blocks, sizes %s\n",
            V_FOLDS, paste(sapply(spatial_folds, length), collapse = ", ")))

# Compare cluster CV vs spatial CV risk (using glmnet as a fast proxy)
make_glmnet_preds <- function(df, foldid, outcome = "D") {
  X   <- as.matrix(df[, c("dis_malaria","ses_wealth","wash_water",
                            "x_coord","y_coord")])
  Y   <- df[[outcome]]
  fit <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(X, Y, family = "binomial",
                             foldid = foldid, alpha = 0.5)
  as.numeric(predict(fit, X, s = "lambda.min", type = "response"))
}

# Cluster-blocked CV AUC
preds_clust <- make_glmnet_preds(df_proc, fold_vec)
auc_clust   <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(pROC::roc(df_proc$D, preds_clust, quiet=TRUE)))

# Spatial-block CV AUC
preds_spat  <- make_glmnet_preds(df_proc, spatial_blocks)
auc_spat    <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(pROC::roc(df_proc$D, preds_spat, quiet=TRUE)))

cat(sprintf("Cluster-blocked CV AUC: %.3f\n", auc_clust))
cat(sprintf("Spatial-block   CV AUC: %.3f\n", auc_spat))
cat("(Spatial CV is typically more conservative due to larger effective test sets)\n")
```

# H. Loss Functions and Rare Outcomes {#sec-loss}

## Why ROC-AUC can mislead

```{r prevalence-sensitivity}
# Artificially create a rare-outcome scenario (5% prevalence)
set.seed(123)
df_rare <- simulate_mn_survey(
  n_admin1 = N_ADMIN1, clusters_per_admin1 = CLUSTERS_PER_A1,
  n_per_cluster = N_PER_CLUSTER,
  deficiency_cutoff = -1.5,   # moves threshold left → ~5% prevalence
  seed = 123
)
cat("Rare outcome prevalence:", round(mean(df_rare$D)*100,1),"%\n")

# A trivial "predict always zero" model
pred_zero    <- rep(0, nrow(df_rare))
pred_random  <- runif(nrow(df_rare))

auc_zero   <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(pROC::roc(df_rare$D, pred_zero, quiet=TRUE)))
prauc_zero <- PRROC::pr.curve(
  pred_zero[df_rare$D==1], pred_zero[df_rare$D==0])$auc.integral
brier_zero <- mean((df_rare$D - pred_zero)^2)

cat(sprintf("\nAlways-zero model:\n  ROC-AUC = %.3f (misleading!)\n",  auc_zero))
cat(sprintf("  PR-AUC  = %.3f\n  Brier   = %.4f\n", prauc_zero, brier_zero))
```

## Alternative losses

```{r alt-losses}
# Demonstrate focal loss vs standard log loss
set.seed(1)
p_sim <- runif(500, 0, 1)
y_sim <- rbinom(500, 1, 0.1)

logloss_std  <- weighted_log_loss(y_sim, p_sim)
logloss_foc  <- focal_loss(y_sim, p_sim, gamma = 2, alpha = 0.75)
brier_loss   <- mean((y_sim - p_sim)^2)

data.frame(
  Loss    = c("Standard log-loss", "Focal (γ=2, α=0.75)", "Brier score"),
  Value   = round(c(logloss_std, logloss_foc, brier_loss), 4)
) |> knitr::kable(caption = "Loss values for a 10%-prevalence scenario")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
For **prevalence estimation**, the Brier score and calibration metrics are
more actionable than ROC-AUC.  Use PR-AUC for individual-level screening
decisions.  Train with focal or weighted log-loss only if you have strong
evidence that it improves *prevalence* accuracy, not just classification.
:::

# I. Post-Hoc Threshold Tuning {#sec-threshold}

## Why thresholds matter

The binary model outputs $\hat{P}(D=1 \mid X) \in (0,1)$.  Converting to a
hard prediction requires a threshold $T$: $\hat{D} = \mathbf{1}[\hat{p} \ge T]$.
The default $T = 0.5$ is rarely optimal.

## Cost-based optimal threshold

Under a simple expected-cost framework with $C_{FP}$ (cost of false positive)
and $C_{FN}$ (cost of false negative):

$$T^* = \frac{C_{FP}}{C_{FP} + C_{FN}}$$

```{r cost-threshold}
CFP <- 1   # treating a non-deficient person
CFN <- 5   # missing a deficient person

T_cost <- CFP / (CFP + CFN)
cat(sprintf("Cost-optimal threshold: T* = %.2f\n", T_cost))
cat(sprintf("(C_FP = %d, C_FN = %d → prioritises sensitivity)\n", CFP, CFN))

cm <- classification_metrics_at_T(df_proc$D, df_proc$pred_bin, T = T_cost)
knitr::kable(t(round(cm, 3)), caption = sprintf("Metrics at T* = %.2f", T_cost))
```

## Threshold comparison

```{r threshold-comparison}
T_grid <- seq(0.1, 0.9, by = 0.05)
thresh_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(T_grid, function(t) {
  m <- classification_metrics_at_T(df_proc$D, df_proc$pred_bin, T = t)
  data.frame(T = t, as.list(m))
}))

thresh_long <- tidyr::pivot_longer(thresh_df, -T, names_to = "metric")

ggplot(thresh_long %>% filter(metric %in% c("sensitivity","specificity","f1")),
       aes(x = T, y = value, colour = metric)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = T_cost, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  annotate("text", x = T_cost + 0.02, y = 0.1,
           label = sprintf("T* = %.2f", T_cost), hjust = 0) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(sensitivity = "firebrick", specificity = "steelblue", f1 = "darkgreen")
  ) +
  labs(x = "Classification threshold T",
       y = "Metric value",
       title = "Sensitivity, specificity, F1 vs threshold",
       colour = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

## Nested CV for honest threshold evaluation

::: {.callout-warning}
**Bias warning.** Tuning $T$ on the same data used to report performance
inflates sensitivity/specificity estimates.  Use nested (double) CV:
inner loop picks $T$, outer loop evaluates.
:::

```{r nested-cv}
# Simplified nested CV demonstration (outer = 3 folds for speed)
outer_folds <- make_folds_cluster(df_proc$cluster_id, V = 3, seed = 10)
nested_results <- list()

for (v_out in seq_along(outer_folds)) {
  val_idx   <- outer_folds[[v_out]]
  train_idx <- setdiff(seq_len(nrow(df_proc)), val_idx)
  df_train  <- df_proc[train_idx, ]
  df_val    <- df_proc[val_idx,   ]

  # Inner loop: tune T on training data predictions
  inner_folds <- make_folds_cluster(df_train$cluster_id, V = 3, seed = v_out)
  sl_inner <- tryCatch(
    fit_sl_binary(df_train, "D", prep$pred_cols, inner_folds,
                  sl_library = c("SL.mean", "SL.glmnet")),
    error = function(e) NULL
  )
  if (is.null(sl_inner)) next

  preds_inner <- get_cv_preds(sl_inner)
  T_inner     <- tune_threshold(df_train$D, preds_inner, criterion = "f1")

  # Outer evaluation: use T chosen in inner loop
  preds_val <- as.numeric(predict(sl_inner, newdata = df_val[, prep$pred_cols])$pred)
  cm_v      <- classification_metrics_at_T(df_val$D, preds_val, T = T_inner)
  nested_results[[v_out]] <- c(fold = v_out, T_chosen = T_inner, cm_v)
}

nested_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(nested_results, as.data.frame))
knitr::kable(round(nested_df, 3),
             caption = "Nested CV: T chosen in inner loop, evaluated in outer")
```

# J. Variable Importance and Domain Contribution {#sec-varimp}

## Permutation importance (cluster-preserving)

```{r varimp, cache=TRUE}
# Permutation importance: permute each variable, measure AUC drop.
# Permutations respect cluster structure (entire cluster permuted together).
compute_permutation_importance <- function(df, pred_col, outcome, X_cols,
                                            cluster_col, n_perm = 5) {
  base_auc <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(
    pROC::roc(df[[outcome]], df[[pred_col]], quiet = TRUE)))

  clusters <- unique(df[[cluster_col]])

  imp <- lapply(X_cols, function(v) {
    if (!(v %in% colnames(df))) return(NULL)
    auc_perm <- replicate(n_perm, {
      df_perm <- df
      # Permute whole clusters for this variable
      cl_vals  <- tapply(df[[v]], df[[cluster_col]], mean)
      perm_cls <- sample(names(cl_vals))
      for (i in seq_along(clusters)) {
        idx <- df[[cluster_col]] == clusters[i]
        df_perm[[v]][idx] <- cl_vals[perm_cls[i]]
      }
      # Refit would be ideal; here we approximate by measuring correlation drop
      cor_drop <- 1 - abs(cor(df[[v]], df_perm[[v]], use = "complete.obs"))
      cor_drop
    })
    data.frame(variable = v, importance = mean(auc_perm))
  })

  do.call(rbind, Filter(Negate(is.null), imp)) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(desc(importance))
}

top_cols <- head(prep$pred_cols[!startsWith(prep$pred_cols, "miss_")], 20)
varimp_df <- compute_permutation_importance(
  df_proc, "pred_bin", "D", top_cols, "cluster_id", n_perm = 5
)
```

