```{r varimp-plot, fig.height=6}
varimp_df$variable <- factor(varimp_df$variable,
                               levels = varimp_df$variable[order(varimp_df$importance)])
ggplot(head(varimp_df, 15),
       aes(x = variable, y = importance)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.85) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Permutation importance (correlation disruption)",
       title = "Top-15 variable importance",
       subtitle = "Higher = greater contribution to predictions")
```

## Grouped domain importance

```{r domain-importance}
domain_grps <- make_domain_groups(df_proc)
domain_imp  <- lapply(names(domain_grps), function(dom) {
  dom_cols <- intersect(domain_grps[[dom]], varimp_df$variable)
  if (length(dom_cols) == 0) return(NULL)
  total <- sum(varimp_df$importance[varimp_df$variable %in% dom_cols])
  data.frame(domain = dom, total_importance = total, n_vars = length(dom_cols))
})
domain_imp_df <- do.call(rbind, Filter(Negate(is.null), domain_imp))
knitr::kable(domain_imp_df %>%
               mutate(total_importance = round(total_importance, 4)) %>%
               arrange(desc(total_importance)),
             caption = "Grouped domain importance (sum of permutation importance)")
```

::: {.callout-caution}
**Interpretation caveats.**
(1) Correlated variables share importance — removing one may not reduce performance.
(2) A domain with more variables mechanically accumulates more total importance.
(3) Permutation importance measures predictive contribution, not causal effect.
:::

# K. Alternative Modeling Families {#sec-alternatives}

## K.1 Spatial GAM (mgcv — stand-in for hierarchical Bayes)

```{r gam-alternative, fig.height=4}
# Already fitted above; predict on grid for comparison
grid_proc$pred_gam <- predict(gam_sp,
                               newdata = grid_proc, type = "response")

p_gam <- ggplot(grid_proc, aes(x_coord, y_coord, fill = pred_gam)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c("P(D=1)", limits = c(0,1), option = "magma") +
  labs(title = "Spatial GAM prediction",
       subtitle = "s(x,y, bs='tp') + covariates") +
  theme_void()

p_sl  <- ggplot(grid_proc, aes(x_coord, y_coord, fill = pred_prob)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c("P(D=1)", limits = c(0,1), option = "magma") +
  labs(title = "Super Learner prediction") +
  theme_void()

p_sl + p_gam
```

## K.2 Fay-Herriot small area estimation

```{r fay-herriot}
# Direct survey estimates per Admin1 (design-based, treating our data as survey)
direct_est <- tapply(df_proc$D, df_proc$admin1_name, mean)
samp_var   <- tapply(df_proc$D, df_proc$admin1_name, function(x) var(x)/length(x))

# Admin1-level covariate design matrix
a1_covs <- df_proc %>%
  group_by(admin1_name) %>%
  summarise(dis_malaria = mean(dis_malaria),
            ses_wealth  = mean(ses_wealth),
            wash_water  = mean(wash_water))

fh_res <- fit_fay_herriot(direct_est, samp_var, a1_covs)

fh_tbl <- data.frame(
  admin1   = names(direct_est),
  direct   = round(direct_est * 100, 1),
  eblup    = round(fh_res$eblup * 100, 1),
  true     = round(true_prev[names(direct_est)] * 100, 1)
)
knitr::kable(fh_tbl,
             caption = "Fay-Herriot EBLUP vs direct estimate vs true prevalence (%)")
```

## K.3 Geostatistical model (empirical variogram + kriging concept)

```{r geostatistical}
# Illustrate the geostatistical approach conceptually using cluster residuals
vario <- empirical_variogram(cl_resid, n_bins = 12)
plot_variogram(vario) +
  labs(subtitle = "Sill and range guide spatial interpolation radius")
```

**Comparison summary:**

| Method | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| Super Learner | Flexible, non-parametric, high predictive accuracy | Black-box, slow bootstrap, needs large $n$ |
| Spatial GAM | Interpretable, posterior uncertainty, spatial smoothing | Assumes additive structure, limited interaction |
| Fay-Herriot | Design-based, shrinks unstable direct estimates | Area-level only, linear linking model |
| Geostatistical | Principled spatial interpolation, kriging SE | Requires stationarity, variogram model choice |

# L. Practical Checklist and Common Failure Modes {#sec-checklist}

## The essential checklist

```{r checklist, echo=FALSE}
checklist <- data.frame(
  Item = c(
    "Cluster-blocked CV (not individual-level randomisation)",
    "Imputation fitted on training data only",
    "Standardisation fitted on training data only",
    "Outcome leakage check (no derived biomarker columns as predictors)",
    "Calibration assessed (slope ≈ 1, intercept ≈ 0)",
    "PR-AUC and Brier score reported alongside ROC-AUC",
    "Bootstrap resamples clusters (not individuals)",
    "Survey weights applied in prevalence aggregation",
    "Residual spatial autocorrelation tested (Moran's I)",
    "Threshold T tuned on separate data (nested CV if tuning needed)"
  ),
  Status = rep("☐", 10)
)
knitr::kable(checklist, caption = "Pre-publication model checklist")
```

## Common failure modes

**1. Outcome leakage in predictors.**
Including derived variables (e.g., haemoglobin from the same survey as ferritin)
as covariates will artificially inflate CV performance.  Always audit domain
prefixes and exclude any variable measured at the same visit as the outcome.

**2. Individual-level CV with cluster data.**
With 15 individuals per cluster, a randomly assigned fold will typically
contain 14 cluster-mates of each validation individual in the training set,
leaking the cluster random effect and over-estimating generalisability.

**3. Geographic identifier inconsistencies.**
Merging cluster GPS to Admin2 boundaries may misclassify border clusters.
Always visualise the spatial join and inspect mismatches before aggregating.

**4. Temporal misalignment of covariates and biomarkers.**
A satellite-derived NDVI raster from a different season than the survey will
attenuate or reverse the expected association.  Match covariate extraction
windows to survey fieldwork dates.

**5. Survey displacement.**
DHS GPS clusters are randomly displaced up to 2 km (urban) or 5 km (rural).
This attenuates spatially-varying covariate associations.  Account for it by
using covariates averaged over a buffer radius, not exact-location extracts.

**6. Model transferability assumptions.**
A model trained in one country is transferable to another only if:
(a) covariate distributions overlap (common support),
(b) covariate–biomarker associations are stable across contexts, and
(c) measurement protocols for both predictors and outcomes are comparable.
Validate explicitly with held-out geographic areas if possible.

## Residual analysis

```{r residual-analysis}
# Final residual check on binary model
df_proc$resid_bin <- df_proc$D - df_proc$pred_bin

p_resid_hist <- ggplot(df_proc, aes(x = resid_bin)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(x = "D - P̂(D=1|X)", title = "Binary residuals")

p_resid_pred <- ggplot(df_proc, aes(x = pred_bin, y = resid_bin)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.8, colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", colour = "firebrick", se = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "Predicted P(D=1)", y = "Residual",
       title = "Residual vs fitted")

p_resid_hist + p_resid_pred
```

## Session information

```{r session-info, echo=FALSE}
cat("R version:", R.version$version.string, "\n")
cat("Key packages:\n")
pkg_versions <- c("SuperLearner","glmnet","ranger","mgcv","sf","spdep","pROC","PRROC")
invisible(lapply(pkg_versions, function(p) {
  v <- tryCatch(as.character(packageVersion(p)), error = function(e) "not installed")
  cat(sprintf("  %-18s %s\n", p, v))
}))
```
